{"step_by_step_thinking": "The p-value is a probability that the observed effect in a study could have occurred by chance if there were no true effect (i.e., if the null hypothesis is true). A p-value of less than 0.10 means that there is less than a 10% probability that the observed result is due to random chance alone. This does not mean that 90% of results could have occurred by chance (which would be option B), nor does it imply that the test is 90% reproducible (option A), or that 90% of patients will benefit (option D). Option C states 'Not more than 10% of the people benefitted by the drug could be due to chance' — this misinterprets the p-value. The p-value refers to the probability of observing the data (or more extreme) if there is no true effect. Therefore, a p-value < 0.10 means that the observed effect is unlikely to be due to chance alone, but it does not mean that less than 10% of patients benefited due to chance.
